topics.title: Graphs
topics.description: A graph is a collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges — the universal data structure for modeling relationships, networks, and reachability problems.
topics.story: A city road map is a graph — intersections are nodes, roads are edges. Facebook's friend network is a graph. The internet is a graph. Graphs are everywhere because they model relationships. Mastering BFS, DFS, Union-Find, and Dijkstra on graphs is what separates engineers who 'can code' from engineers who 'can solve problems'.
topics.analogy: A graph is like a flight network: cities are nodes, direct flights are edges. Finding the cheapest route from Delhi to New York (weighted shortest path) is Dijkstra. Checking if you can fly from any city to any other (connectivity) is Union-Find or BFS.
topics.memoryAnchor: BFS = ripples in water = shortest path (unweighted). DFS = going deep first = cycle detection, topological sort. Dijkstra = greedy BFS with priority queue = shortest path (weighted). Union-Find = merge circles of friends.
topics.firstPrinciples: Represent as adjacency list (Map<Integer, List<Integer>>) for sparse graphs, adjacency matrix (int[][]) for dense graphs. V = vertices, E = edges. BFS/DFS: O(V+E). Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V). Union-Find: O(α(V)) ≈ O(1). Always track visited to avoid infinite loops.
topics.bruteForce: For connectivity: try all paths between every pair — O(V * (V+E)). For shortest path: BFS from source, stop at target — already optimal for unweighted. For cycle detection: try all DFS paths — already O(V+E).
topics.optimizedApproach: Build adjacency list first. Always maintain a visited set/array. For grid problems, treat each cell as a node. For shortest path in unweighted graph → BFS. For weighted → Dijkstra. For cycle → DFS with grey/black coloring.
topics.whenToUse: Any problem involving nodes and connections: number of islands (grid graph), course schedule (directed graph + topological sort), network delay (weighted graph + Dijkstra), social network (undirected + BFS), word ladder (BFS on implicit graph).

examples.title: BFS — Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":12,"lineCode":"queue.offer(src); visited.add(src);","variables":{"queue":"[1]","visited":"{1}","steps":0},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Seed the queue with the source node. Mark it visited immediately to avoid re-queuing."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"int size = queue.size();","variables":{"size":1,"steps":0},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"Level 0: one node (src=1). We process all nodes at the same BFS level before incrementing steps."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"steps++;","variables":{"steps":1},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Moving to level 1. Every node dequeued in this iteration is exactly 1 hop from src."},{"line":23,"lineCode":"int node = queue.poll();","variables":{"node":1,"queue":"[]"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Dequeue node 1. Process its neighbors: 2 and 3."},{"line":25,"lineCode":"if (neighbor == dst) return steps;","variables":{"neighbor":2,"steps":1},"phase":"BRANCH","annotation":"Neighbor 2 ≠ dst(6). Add to queue."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"visited.add(neighbor); queue.offer(neighbor);","variables":{"queue":"[2,3]","visited":"{1,2,3}"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Level 1 complete: queue holds [2,3]. Both are 1 hop from source."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"steps++;","variables":{"steps":2},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"Level 2: processing nodes [2,3] which are 2 hops from src."},{"line":25,"lineCode":"if (neighbor == dst) return steps;","variables":{"neighbor":6,"steps":3},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"At level 3, node 4 or 5 yields neighbor 6 == dst. Return steps=3. BFS guarantees this is the shortest path."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Start: enqueue src, visited=src] --> B[Queue empty?]
    B -->|yes| Z[Return -1 unreachable]
    B -->|no| C[Record level size]
    C --> D[Dequeue node]
    D --> E{neighbor == dst?}
    E -->|yes| F[Return current steps]
    E -->|no| G{Already visited?}
    G -->|yes| H[Skip]
    G -->|no| I[Mark visited + enqueue]
    H --> J{More in level?}
    I --> J
    J -->|yes| D
    J -->|no| K[steps++]
    K --> B
examples.pseudocode:
BFS(graph, src, dst):
  queue = [src]
  visited = {src}
  steps = 0

  WHILE queue not empty:
    FOR each node at current level:
      node = dequeue()
      FOR each neighbour of node:
        IF neighbour == dst → return steps
        IF neighbour not in visited:
          visited.add(neighbour)
          enqueue(neighbour)
    steps++

  return -1  ← dst unreachable
examples.explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level — all nodes 1 hop away before any node 2 hops away. This level-by-level guarantee is why BFS gives the shortest path in unweighted graphs. The key pattern: process ALL nodes at the current level before incrementing steps. The visited set prevents re-queuing the same node, keeping complexity O(V+E).
examples.realWorldUse: Google Maps 'fewer transfers' mode uses BFS on a transit graph. P2P networks (BitTorrent) use BFS to find the nearest peers holding a file chunk. Social networks use BFS to find 'degrees of separation' between users. Crawlers use BFS to discover web pages layer by layer.

examples.title: DFS — Cycle Detection in Directed Graph
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":21,"lineCode":"color[node] = 1;","variables":{"node":0,"color":"[1,0,0]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Mark node 0 GREY (on current DFS path). Grey means: we're currently exploring this node's subtree."},{"line":23,"lineCode":"if (color[nb] == 1) return true;","variables":{"nb":1,"color[1]":0},"phase":"BRANCH","annotation":"Neighbor 1 is WHITE (unvisited). No cycle yet. Recurse into node 1."},{"line":21,"lineCode":"color[node] = 1;","variables":{"node":1,"color":"[1,1,0]"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Mark node 1 GREY. Stack is now: 0→1."},{"line":21,"lineCode":"color[node] = 1;","variables":{"node":2,"color":"[1,1,1]"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Mark node 2 GREY. Stack: 0→1→2."},{"line":23,"lineCode":"if (color[nb] == 1) return true;","variables":{"nb":0,"color[0]":1},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"CYCLE FOUND! Node 2's neighbor is node 0, which is GREY (already on the current path). This is a back-edge — the definition of a cycle."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"color[node] = 2;","variables":{"node":1,"color":"[1,2,2]"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"(Acyclic path: after fully exploring node 1 with no cycle) mark BLACK. BLACK = safe, no cycle through this node."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[dfs node] --> B[Color node GREY]
    B --> C[For each neighbor]
    C --> D{Neighbor GREY?}
    D -->|yes| E[CYCLE FOUND! return true]
    D -->|no| F{Neighbor WHITE?}
    F -->|yes| G[Recurse dfs neighbor]
    G --> H{returned true?}
    H -->|yes| E
    H -->|no| C
    F -->|no BLACK| C
    C -->|all done| I[Color node BLACK]
    I --> J[return false]
examples.pseudocode:
hasCycle(n, edges):
  color[0..n-1] = WHITE
  build adjacency list

  FOR each node i:
    IF color[i] == WHITE:
      IF dfs(i) → return true
  return false

dfs(node):
  color[node] = GREY        ← on current path
  FOR each neighbor:
    IF color[nb] == GREY → CYCLE (back-edge)
    IF color[nb] == WHITE → recurse
  color[node] = BLACK       ← fully safe
  return false
examples.explanation: Three-color DFS is the canonical cycle detector for directed graphs. WHITE = not yet visited. GREY = currently on the DFS call stack (part of our exploration path). BLACK = fully processed with no cycle. A cycle exists if and only if we encounter a GREY neighbor — meaning we've looped back to something already on our current path. This is more powerful than simple visited-set tracking (which works for undirected graphs but not directed).
examples.realWorldUse: Build systems (Maven, Gradle) use this to detect circular dependencies between modules — a cycle means 'A needs B, B needs A' and the build can never start. Package managers (npm, pip) run cycle detection before installing. Java's class loader detects circular class dependencies this way.

examples.title: Topological Sort — Kahn's BFS Algorithm
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":13,"lineCode":"inDegree[e[1]]++;","variables":{"inDegree":"[0,0,2,1,2]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"After processing all edges: node 2 has 2 incoming (from 0 and 1), node 4 has 2 incoming (from 2 and 3). Nodes with inDegree=0 have no prerequisites."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"if (inDegree[i] == 0) queue.offer(i);","variables":{"queue":"[0,1]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Nodes 0 and 1 have no prerequisites — they can be taken first. Seed the BFS queue with them."},{"line":23,"lineCode":"int node = queue.poll();","variables":{"node":0,"order":"[0]"},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"Process node 0. Add to ordering."},{"line":26,"lineCode":"if (--inDegree[nb] == 0)","variables":{"nb":2,"inDegree[2]":1},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Decrement inDegree of node 2 (from 2 to 1). Still has 1 prerequisite (node 1), not ready yet."},{"line":23,"lineCode":"int node = queue.poll();","variables":{"node":1,"order":"[0,1]"},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"Process node 1. Its neighbors are 2 and 3."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"queue.offer(nb);","variables":{"nb":2,"inDegree[2]":0,"queue":"[3,2]"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Node 2's inDegree hits 0 — all prerequisites done. Add to queue. Node 3 also gets added."},{"line":30,"lineCode":"return order.size() == n ? order : List.of();","variables":{"order.size":5,"n":5},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"All 5 nodes processed → no cycle. Return topological order [0,1,3,2,4] (or [0,1,2,3,4] depending on queue order)."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Build adjacency list + compute in-degrees] --> B[Enqueue all nodes with in-degree 0]
    B --> C{Queue empty?}
    C -->|yes| D{order.size == n?}
    D -->|yes| E[Return valid topological order]
    D -->|no| F[Return empty — cycle detected]
    C -->|no| G[Dequeue node → add to order]
    G --> H[For each neighbor]
    H --> I[Decrement neighbor in-degree]
    I --> J{in-degree == 0?}
    J -->|yes| K[Enqueue neighbor]
    J -->|no| H
    K --> H
    H -->|done| C
examples.pseudocode:
topoSort(n, edges):
  build adj list
  inDegree[i] = count of edges pointing TO i

  queue = all nodes with inDegree == 0
  order = []

  WHILE queue not empty:
    node = dequeue()
    order.append(node)
    FOR each neighbor of node:
      inDegree[neighbor]--
      IF inDegree[neighbor] == 0:
        enqueue(neighbor)

  IF order.size == n → return order  (no cycle)
  ELSE → return []  (cycle detected)
examples.explanation: Kahn's algorithm processes nodes in dependency order. A node is 'ready' when all its prerequisites have been processed (in-degree drops to 0). The cycle-detection is elegant: if any node is part of a cycle, its in-degree can never reach 0 (because its cycle partner always has it as a prerequisite), so it never enters the queue. The final check `order.size == n` catches this.
examples.realWorldUse: Build systems (Make, Gradle) compile files in topological order — compile dependencies before dependents. npm install resolves package install order. Operating system task schedulers. CPU instruction scheduling in compilers. Database query optimizers plan JOIN execution order using topological sort.

examples.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm — Weighted Shortest Path
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":12,"lineCode":"dist[src] = 0;","variables":{"dist":"[0, MAX, MAX, MAX]","pq":"[(0,0)]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Start: distance to source is 0, everything else is infinity. Heap has just the source."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"int[] curr = pq.poll();","variables":{"d":0,"u":0,"pq":"[]"},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"Pop cheapest: node 0, distance 0. Process its edges."},{"line":26,"lineCode":"if (newDist < dist[v])","variables":{"v":1,"newDist":4,"dist[1]":"MAX"},"phase":"BRANCH","annotation":"Edge 0→1 (w=4): 0+4=4 < MAX. Update dist[1]=4, push (4,1)."},{"line":26,"lineCode":"if (newDist < dist[v])","variables":{"v":2,"newDist":1,"dist[2]":"MAX"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Edge 0→2 (w=1): 0+1=1 < MAX. Update dist[2]=1, push (1,2). Dist now [0,4,1,MAX]."},{"line":19,"lineCode":"int[] curr = pq.poll();","variables":{"d":1,"u":2,"pq":"[(4,1)]"},"phase":"LOOP","annotation":"Pop cheapest: (1,2). Process node 2's edges."},{"line":26,"lineCode":"if (newDist < dist[v])","variables":{"v":1,"newDist":3,"dist[1]":4},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Edge 2→1 (w=2): 1+2=3 < 4. Better path found! Update dist[1]=3, push (3,1). Dist: [0,3,1,MAX]."},{"line":21,"lineCode":"if (d > dist[u]) continue;","variables":{"d":4,"u":1,"dist[1]":3},"phase":"BRANCH","annotation":"Stale entry (4,1) popped — we already found a better path to node 1 (dist=3). Skip this entry."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"return dist;","variables":{"dist":"[0,3,1,4]"},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Final shortest distances from node 0: to 1=3, to 2=1, to 3=4."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Init: dist=INF, dist[src]=0] --> B[Push src to min-heap]
    B --> C{Heap empty?}
    C -->|yes| Z[Return dist array]
    C -->|no| D[Pop min-cost node u]
    D --> E{d > dist[u]?}
    E -->|yes stale| C
    E -->|no| F[For each neighbor v of u]
    F --> G[newDist = dist[u] + weight]
    G --> H{newDist < dist[v]?}
    H -->|yes| I[Update dist[v] = newDist]
    I --> J[Push newDist,v to heap]
    J --> F
    H -->|no| F
    F -->|done| C
examples.pseudocode:
Dijkstra(graph, src):
  dist[all] = INF
  dist[src] = 0
  minHeap = [(0, src)]

  WHILE heap not empty:
    (d, u) = pop minimum
    IF d > dist[u] → skip (stale)

    FOR each (v, w) in neighbors of u:
      newDist = dist[u] + w
      IF newDist < dist[v]:
        dist[v] = newDist
        push (newDist, v) to heap

  return dist
examples.explanation: Dijkstra is greedy BFS with a twist: instead of processing nodes level by level, it always processes the globally cheapest unvisited node next. The min-heap ensures this. The stale-entry check (`d > dist[u]`) is critical — when a shorter path is found, an old entry remains in the heap; skip it. Dijkstra only works with non-negative edge weights. For negative weights, use Bellman-Ford.
examples.realWorldUse: Google Maps routing uses Dijkstra (or A* which is Dijkstra + heuristic) for driving directions. Router protocols (OSPF) run Dijkstra to build shortest-path trees for IP packet routing. Flight booking engines compute cheapest itineraries. Network distance-vector routing protocols.

examples.title: Union-Find — Connected Components with Path Compression
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":10,"lineCode":"for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = i;","variables":{"parent":"[0,1,2,3,4,5]","components":6},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Initially each node is its own root/leader. 6 separate components."},{"line":22,"lineCode":"int rx = find(x), ry = find(y);","variables":{"x":0,"y":1,"rx":0,"ry":1},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Different roots → merge."},{"line":29,"lineCode":"parent[ry] = rx; rank[rx]++;","variables":{"parent":"[0,0,2,3,4,5]","rank":"[1,0,0,0,0,0]"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Same rank: attach node 1 under node 0. Increment rank[0] to 1. Components: 5."},{"line":22,"lineCode":"int rx = find(x), ry = find(y);","variables":{"x":1,"y":2,"rx":0,"ry":2},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"union(1,2): find(1) follows parent[1]=0, returns 0. find(2)=2. Different roots."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"parent[rx] = ry;","variables":{"parent":"[0,0,2,3,4,5]"},"phase":"BRANCH","annotation":"rank[0]=1 > rank[2]=0: attach root 2 under root 0. parent[2]=0. Components: 4."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"parent[x] = find(parent[x]);","variables":{"x":2,"parent[2]":0},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"PATH COMPRESSION: when find(2) is called again, parent[2] is already 0. On deeper chains, this flattens the tree so future finds are O(1)."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[union x y] --> B[rx = find x]
    A --> C[ry = find y]
    B --> D{rx == ry?}
    C --> D
    D -->|yes same component| E[return false no-op]
    D -->|no| F{Compare ranks}
    F -->|rank rx < rank ry| G[parent rx = ry]
    F -->|rank rx > rank ry| H[parent ry = rx]
    F -->|equal| I[parent ry = rx, rank rx++]
    G --> J[components--]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[return true merged]

    L[find x] --> M{parent x == x?}
    M -->|yes root| N[return x]
    M -->|no| O[parent x = find parent x]
    O --> N
examples.pseudocode:
find(x):
  IF parent[x] != x:
    parent[x] = find(parent[x])  ← path compression
  return parent[x]

union(x, y):
  rx = find(x)
  ry = find(y)
  IF rx == ry → return false  ← already connected

  // Union by rank
  IF rank[rx] < rank[ry] → parent[rx] = ry
  ELSE IF rank[rx] > rank[ry] → parent[ry] = rx
  ELSE → parent[ry] = rx; rank[rx]++

  components--
  return true
examples.explanation: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) tracks which nodes belong to the same connected component. Path compression flattens the tree during find — after calling find(x), parent[x] points directly to the root, making future finds O(1). Union by rank prevents tall trees from forming. Together they achieve O(α(n)) per operation — nearly constant. The key insight: you don't need to know the exact members of a component, just whether two nodes share a root.
examples.realWorldUse: Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree algorithm uses Union-Find to detect if adding an edge creates a cycle (merge if different components). Percolation theory (does water flow through a porous material?) — Union-Find checks connectivity. Git's merge detection uses a similar structure. Photo organization: grouping faces by identity (same person → same component).

problems.title: Number of Islands
problems.description:
Count the number of islands in a 2D grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water).

**Example 1:**
grid = [[1,1,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,1]] => 2

**Key insight:** DFS from each unvisited '1', mark all connected land as visited. Each DFS start = one island.
problems.hint1: How do you mark land cells you've already counted so you don't count the same island twice?
problems.hint2: For each unvisited '1', start a BFS/DFS to mark all connected '1's as visited (or change to '0'). Each BFS/DFS call = one island.
problems.hint3: DFS: mark grid[r][c]='0', recurse on 4 neighbors if they're '1' and in bounds. Count how many times you initiate a DFS.
problems.constraints: m == grid.length | n == grid[i].length | 1 ≤ m, n ≤ 300 | grid[i][j] is '0' or '1'
problems.editorial: Grid DFS/BFS: O(m*n) time and space. Modifying the grid in-place avoids extra visited array. Each cell is processed at most once. BFS alternative: use a queue, same complexity.

problems.title: Clone Graph
problems.description:
Clone a connected undirected graph. Each node has a val and list of neighbors.

**Example:**
Input: Node(1) connected to Node(2), Node(2) connected to Node(1)
Output: Deep copy with same adjacency

**Key insight:** BFS/DFS with a HashMap<original, clone> to avoid infinite loops and enable reconnecting.
problems.hint1: How do you avoid infinite loops when cloning a graph with cycles?
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap from original node → cloned node. Before recursing, check if the node is already cloned.
problems.hint3: DFS: if node in map → return map.get(node). Else create clone, add to map, then for each neighbor recurse and add to clone's neighbors.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ Node.val ≤ 100 | No repeated edges, no self-loops | Graph is connected
problems.editorial: HashMap acts as both visited set and clone registry. The key insight: add node to map BEFORE recursing into neighbors to handle cycles. O(V+E) time and space.

problems.title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection)
problems.description:
Detect if you can finish all courses given prerequisite pairs (detect cycle in directed graph).

**Example 1:**
canFinish(2, [[1,0]]) => true (take 0 then 1)
canFinish(2, [[1,0],[0,1]]) => false (circular dependency)

**Key insight:** Topological sort (Kahn's algorithm or DFS). Cycle = not all nodes processed.
problems.hint1: This is a cycle detection problem in a directed graph. If there's a cycle, you can't finish all courses.
problems.hint2: Use Kahn's algorithm (BFS topological sort): compute in-degrees, process nodes with in-degree 0, if all nodes are processed → no cycle.
problems.hint3: Kahn's: if result.size() == numCourses after BFS → no cycle, return true.
problems.editorial: Kahn's BFS topological sort detects cycles naturally: if any node is never added to the queue (always has in-degree > 0 due to cycle), processed < numCourses. O(V+E) time and space.

problems.title: Network Delay Time (Dijkstra)
problems.description:
Find the shortest travel time from node k to all other nodes using Dijkstra's algorithm.

**Example:**
times=[[2,1,1],[2,3,1],[3,4,1]], n=4, k=2
=> Answer is max of all shortest paths = 2 (all nodes reachable in ≤ 2 steps)

**Key insight:** Min-heap Dijkstra: (dist, node). Relax edges when shorter path found.
problems.hint1: This is single-source shortest path from node k. Which algorithm handles weighted directed graphs?
problems.hint2: Dijkstra: use a min-heap of [distance, node]. Initialize dist[k]=0, all others infinity.
problems.hint3: After Dijkstra, find max(dist). If any dist == infinity → return -1.
problems.editorial: Dijkstra from source k. Stale entry check: if d > dist[u], skip. After completing, max of dist[] is the answer. O((V+E) log V) time.

problems.title: Number of Connected Components
problems.description:
Count the number of connected components in an undirected graph.

**Example:**
n=5, edges=[[0,1],[1,2],[3,4]] => 2 components ({0,1,2} and {3,4})

**Key insight:** Union-Find or BFS/DFS. Each time we start a new BFS/DFS on an unvisited node, it's a new component.
problems.hint1: A connected component is a group where any two nodes can reach each other.
problems.hint2: Union-Find: start with n components. For each edge, union the two nodes. If different components, decrement count.
problems.hint3: Initialize parent[i]=i, components=n. For each edge [u,v]: if find(u)!=find(v), union and components--.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 2000 | 1 ≤ edges.length ≤ 5000 | No repeated edges, no self-loops
problems.editorial: Union-Find with path compression and union by rank. O(E * α(n)) total. Alternative BFS/DFS: O(V+E) but requires adjacency list.

problems.title: Word Ladder
problems.description:
Find the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord, changing one letter at a time (each word must be in wordList).

**Example 1:**
wordLadder("hit","cog",["hot","dot","dog","lot","log","cog"]) => 5
hit->hot->dot->dog->cog

**Key insight:** BFS from beginWord. At each step, try all 26 letter substitutions. Each valid word is a BFS neighbor.
problems.hint1: This is a shortest path problem. Nodes are words, edges connect words differing by one letter.
problems.hint2: BFS from beginWord. Generate all 26*L variants per word and check against wordSet.
problems.hint3: Remove words from wordSet on first visit to avoid revisiting. Level = transformation count.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ beginWord.length ≤ 10 | 1 ≤ wordList.length ≤ 5000 | All words same length, lowercase letters
problems.editorial: BFS on implicit graph. Generate 26*L variants per word. O(26 * L * N) time where N = wordList size.
